title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.articleTitle: The Psychology of Urban Green Spaces
content.text: In recent years, the importance of urban parks and gardens has (0) come to the fore of urban planning. As cities become more densely populated, the psychological impact of green spaces is being (1) studied by researchers worldwide. It is now widely (2) that even a short walk in a park can significantly reduce stress levels and improve mental well-being. This phenomenon is often (3) to the 'biophilia hypothesis,' which suggests that humans have an innate connection to the natural world. Urban green spaces provide a much-needed (4) from the noise and frantic pace of city life, allowing residents to recharge their mental batteries. Beyond individual health, these areas also (5) social cohesion by providing a common ground for people from diverse backgrounds to interact. However, the challenge for modern architects is to (6) nature into the very fabric of the city, rather than treating it as an afterthought. Vertical gardens and green rooftops are becoming increasingly (7) as viable solutions for limited space. As we look to the future, the integration of greenery will be (8) to creating sustainable and livable urban environments that support the holistic health of all their inhabitants across the globe.

example.explanation: “Come to the fore” is a fixed idiom meaning to become prominent or important.

questions.explanation: “Extensively studied” is a common academic collocation for thorough research.
“Acknowledged” is used when a fact is generally accepted as true.
“Attributed to” means that something is caused by or stems from a specific source.
“Respite” is a formal word for a short period of rest or relief from something difficult.
“Promote social cohesion” is a standard sociological collocation.
“Weave into the fabric” is a common metaphor for integrating something deeply.
“Prevalent” means widespread in a particular area or at a particular time.
“Crucial to” is used to emphasize that something is extremely important for a result.
